serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one cup or glass), as typically served in a Singapore café or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Lemongrass tea, brewed; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; estimated from lemongrass infusion and lime juice components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for lemongrass lime tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for brewed herbal tea (FDC ID 171917, 'Tea, herbal, brewed') and fresh lime juice (FDC ID 167747). Lemongrass lime tea as served in Singapore restaurants is primarily water with trace amounts of lemongrass volatile compounds and a small quantity of lime juice; sugar content varies by restaurant. Values represent an unsweetened or lightly sweetened preparation. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of herbal teas and low-carbohydrate beverages — analogous estimate based on plain herbal tea (GI ~1) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lemongrass lime tea. Analogous estimate based on plain herbal tea and low-sugar infused beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database. Lemongrass lime tea contains negligible digestible carbohydrates (approximately 0.9 g per 100 ml in unsweetened form), resulting in a glycemic load of effectively zero per serving. GI is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) per convention for essentially zero-GI beverages. If sugar is added at the restaurant, GI and GL would increase proportionally.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~2 mg per 100 ml); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Lemongrass lime tea is suitable for individuals with hypertension as it contains negligible sodium (~5 mg per 250 ml serving); however, patients should request unsweetened preparations and be aware that some restaurants may add sugar syrups that do not affect blood pressure directly but contribute to overall caloric load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate and sugar content in unsweetened form; negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Unsweetened lemongrass lime tea has a negligible glycemic load (GL ~0) and is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; patients should specify no added sugar when ordering, as sweetened versions served in Singapore restaurants may contain 15–25 g of added sugar per serving, which would significantly raise the glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium; lemongrass contains antioxidant compounds
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lemongrass lime tea is cardiovascular-friendly in its unsweetened form, containing no saturated fat, no cholesterol, and minimal sodium; lemongrass (Cymbopogon citratus) contains citral and other antioxidant compounds that have been studied for potential lipid-modulating effects, though clinical evidence in humans remains preliminary.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lemongrass Lime Tea (香茅青柠茶, Teh Serai Limau) is a refreshing herbal infusion made by steeping fresh or dried lemongrass stalks in hot water and finishing with freshly squeezed lime juice or lime slices. It is served both hot and cold in Singapore cafés, Chinese restaurants, and hot pot establishments such as Hai Kah Lang. The beverage has a bright, citrusy aroma from the lemongrass essential oils (primarily citral) combined with the tart, zesty flavour of lime. The taste profile is clean, slightly grassy, and mildly sour, with a refreshing finish. In its unsweetened form it is essentially calorie-free, containing approximately 4 kcal per 100 ml, negligible protein, no fat, and under 1 g of carbohydrate per 100 ml. Vitamin C is present in small amounts from the lime juice (~2.5 mg per 100 ml). The glycemic load per serving is effectively zero when unsweetened, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Lemongrass has been used in traditional Southeast Asian and Chinese herbal medicine for digestive support and as a mild diuretic. The drink is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. In Singapore's Chinese restaurant context it is commonly listed as a house beverage or herbal drink alongside chrysanthemum tea and barley water. Nutritional values vary significantly if sugar syrup or honey is added at the point of service.
common_user_queries: Is lemongrass lime tea good for diabetics?
How many calories in lemongrass lime tea?
Is lemongrass lime tea suitable for hypertension?
Lemongrass lime tea nutrition facts Singapore
Can I drink lemongrass tea if I have high blood pressure?
What does lemongrass lime tea taste like?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for lemongrass lime tea was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for brewed herbal tea and fresh lime juice components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain herbal tea. Confidence is medium rather than low because the food is a simple aqueous infusion with well-understood ingredient profiles, and the estimation basis is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171917, herbal tea brewed; FDC ID 167747, lime juice raw) as no exact Singapore-specific entry exists for lemongrass lime tea in HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values represent an unsweetened or very lightly sweetened preparation. 2. fat_g is naturally zero as lemongrass lime tea is an aqueous herbal infusion containing no lipid-bearing ingredients in meaningful quantities. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for brewed herbal tea. 3. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as the plant solids are not consumed in a strained infusion. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is naturally zero as this is a plant-based beverage with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is naturally zero as lemongrass and lime contain no vitamin D. 5. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no meaningful glycemic carbohydrates in unsweetened form; gi_category low per convention for essentially zero-GI beverages. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 confirmed. 6. Fields set to -1 (not reported / not applicable): vitamin_a_mcg_rae — not reported in USDA herbal tea entry; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — not reported; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — not reported; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — not reported; vitamin_b6_mg — not reported; folate_mcg_dfe — not reported; vitamin_e_mg — not reported; phosphorus_mg — not reported; magnesium_mg — not reported; zinc_mg — not reported; selenium_mcg — not reported. These micronutrients are not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entry for brewed herbal tea and are not expected to be nutritionally significant in this beverage. 7. The restaurant Hai Kah Lang (Funan Mall) is a Chinese hot pot establishment; lemongrass lime tea is a common house beverage in this context. Price SGD 7.09 is preserved from the input record. 8. Sweetened versions served in restaurants may contain 15–25 g added sugar per 250 ml serving, which would substantially alter energy, carbohydrate, sugar, GI, and GL values. 9. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301103 verified as a real FoodOn class for tea beverage; used as closest parent. FoodEx2 code AN000A verified as Tea and herbal infusions category. SNOMED CT 74242007 verified as Tea (substance).
